items.correct: No — an alloy is a mixture; its components are not chemically bonded in a fixed ratio and the proportions can be varied.
items.misconception: Alloys are compounds because the metals are chemically joined together.
items.random_wrong: Alloys are pure elements that have simply been melted.
items.why: Mixing molten metals looks like a chemical change, so students assume bonding in fixed ratios; alloy compositions can actually be tuned freely.

items.q: Are pure metals harder than alloys?
items.correct: Usually softer — the identical atoms in a pure metal form regular layers that slide over each other easily; alloying disrupts these layers.
items.misconception: Pure metals are harder because they have no impurities to weaken them.
items.random_wrong: Pure metals are harder because their atoms are heavier than alloy atoms.
items.why: Students assume 'pure' means 'better and stronger'; mechanically, the disruption caused by foreign atoms is what resists deformation.

items.q: Do plastics rot away quickly in landfill?
items.correct: No — most plastics are non-biodegradable; microorganisms cannot break them down, so they persist for very many years.
items.misconception: Buried plastics decompose within a few years like food waste does.
items.random_wrong: Plastics slowly evaporate until they disappear completely.
items.why: Students generalise from organic waste; the strong covalent backbones of synthetic polymers are not food for decomposers.

items.q: Why are alloys usually harder than pure metals?
items.correct: Different-sized atoms disrupt the layers so they cannot slide easily
items.misconception: Alloys contain stronger covalent bonds throughout
items.random_wrong: Alloy atoms are all identical in size
items.why: Correct answer: Different-sized atoms disrupt the layers so they cannot slide easily. “Alloys contain stronger covalent bonds throughout” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Steel is an alloy of iron and:
items.why: Correct answer: Carbon. “Aluminium” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: Many ethene monomers joining into long chains
items.misconception: Ethene molecules dissolving in each other
items.why: It's Many ethene monomers joining into long chains. “Ethene molecules dissolving in each other” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Metals conduct electricity because they have:
items.correct: Delocalised electrons that are free to move
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Delocalised electrons that are free to move. “Free-moving protons” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
